sentences.text: Good morning.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
No.
When I--
I come from Rhodes Island. 1944, the Germans took us, you know.
The-- just try and-- before the Germans--
Oh.
Yeah.
OK.
Yes, I understand.
Yeah, OK.
Yeah.
All right.
I was a young girl.
We were four sisters and three brothers.
They names there were-- [INAUDIBLE], the first it was-- we were eight, you know.
One died, so we remained seven.
One was Djoya.
My sister, the eldest.
And then Isaac.
And then Caden.
And then Lucia is me.
And then my sister Felicie.
And then my brother Matteo.
And then my brother Maurice, who died also.
My dad is Benjamin, Benyamin.
And my mother was Rosa.
Yes.
My father came from Turkey, you see, to Rhodes.
And he was a wealthy man.
And then with his brothers, he became a partner of [INAUDIBLE] stores.
So I think for a few years, he lost all the money, you see.
And then he had a store of dry foods to sell.
And he was going on the life, you see.
And we were very happy family.
My mother she was very nice.
She was a house--
Housewife.
--housewife, you see.
So we were in the school. 1933, my sister married an Italian.
So they didn't like, because they married in Italian fellow.
So they say, you can't stay in the-- in the Jewish school.
So I came out when I was about 13 from the school.
No, they said that you can't come to school, because your sister got married to an Italian.
Yeah, the school.
Yeah, the family.
So my-- my three-- the-- my two brothers and the sister, she went to the Italian school.
And I came out.
I didn't go to school again.
So I went to work in this factory, this cigarette factory, you see.
And I was there happy.
And after when my mother died in 1936-- What did she die of?
She just was sick.
And she had an operation, you see, or arrhythmia, you see.
And when she was ready to come home, the operation-- the, what you call that thingy?
Open, there-- where she had the operation?
The stitches broke out.
And then they made her again the operation.
And after a few months, she died, you know.
So I was crying a lot, because my mother she was a very nice lady.
And then after that, I still work in the factory of cigarettes, you see.
My brother still was young, you know.
And the last, Maurizio, was very young.
My sister Djoya took us care now.
In 1938, the Jews they were running away from Budapest.
They came to Rhodes, you see.
That's why I want to tell you about my brothers when they went to Israel in 1938.
We, no, just nice.
Not very religious, no.
You see my brother used to-- yes, my father used to go to synagogue every day, night, and all the time, you see.
And we, the young kids, we didn't go very much like here and Cape Town, you see.
They used to go, the old people.
My mother, she used to go, you see.
And Saturday, she never-- she used to smoke.
She never smoked.
She never did nothing.
Yeah, we respected Shabbat, you know.
And after when my mother died, the-- the house without the mother, you know how it is.
It was very sad.
I used to cry all the time when I used to come from work, you know?
And then the life was going on.
And what we had, you know, we-- when my father came from Turkey, he brought plenty money, you see, old money.
So my sister all the time, she used to change the money.
And we used to live with that thing.
And plus, my father used to bring the-- you know.
1938, you see, they said that we-- the Italian, they mustn't talk to the Jewish people.
Because it's the racism was coming, you know, on.
And so, but all the time, the Italian, they were so nice people.
They like the Jewish people in Rhodes.
And we used to talk.
And they used to tell us there's people killing in Russia and things we didn't believe.
Yeah, we knew a little bit.
But we didn't know it was so hard, you know?
Yeah.
Yes.
To Rhodes, yeah.
We never thought so.
Because the Germans, they were already in Rhodes.
And they didn't touch no Jews at all.
They were walking down, buying from Jewish people things.
They never did nothing.
Yeah, we were free.
1938, you know, the Jews, they were running away from Budapest.
They came to Rhodes.
So they had a boat.
And then, all of a sudden, it took fire, you know?
So they stay in-- in the stadium.
The Italians took them out from that boat.
They put them in the stadium.
And all the Jews of Rhodes, we took them food every day.
So I used to go with the bicycle, you know, and take the food to them.
And [? one ?] [INAUDIBLE], because we didn't know other language ourselves, only Italian, French, Greek, and Spanish, you see?
They were talking German.
They were talking Yiddish.
We didn't understand.
But they had little books, you know, and they were asking questions which to tell them.
So they-- they were going to Israel.
So after they went away, my two brothers decided to go together.
Many Jews of Rhodes who had Italian passport, they went with this boat.
And the other ones, they emigrated.
Because the Italian, they say, you-- you haven't got Italian passport.
You must go away from Rhodes.
So many people, they went to Tangier, you know.
And they saved themselves.
Eh?
Yes.
They were running, because-- they were running, because of the Germans.
They say so.
And they-- they run away.
And they left all their families and the houses, you know.
Many-- they were young fellows, not very old people, you see?
Yes.
Yeah.
Yeah, two brothers.
One was 14.
Matteo was 14.
And the other brother was about 25-- something like this.
Nothing.
We are crying when they left.
And then we didn't know nothing, because they-- when they arrive in Israel, they couldn't go in also.
Because the English people were there.
They didn't let them go free.
So they wait till 4 o'clock in the morning to run through the-- you know?
The [SPANISH].
Yeah.
When they-- they just went in the night-- 4 o'clock in the night, they came out of the-- [NON-ENGLISH] you know?
And they just went to Israel, there.
And they were so happy there.
And then we start getting letters, you know?
No, we were in Trianta.
It's a big village.
Everybody went there, because they were throwing bombs in Rhodes, you know?
So we was happy there.
And we had a nice time with the Greek people.
So one day, they say-- I went to Rhodes.
You know, it was not so much, only with the bicycle for one hour, half an hour, I don't remember.
So they say all the Jewish men, they must go for a meeting.
So they went-- Who told you that?
The people, they told me that they call these men, you know, for a meeting.
Only the men, they must go for a meeting.
The President, you know, Mr.
Franco.
It was Mr.
Franco, I think.
He called them.
And all the people went.
So my father was dead already.
So my brother Maurizio went there.
And all the other people, you see?
After it was nighttime, about 7 o'clock in the night, they say they mustn't they don't-- they don't let them come out.
Because they must call their wives and the family with all the gold and the money to go-- to go and meet them.
So the poor people-- so everybody took things-- a suitcase, and they put a few things and the money and the gold what they had.
They arrived there in that-- in that-- what do you call?
It was the airport, you know, Italian airport.
And we went there all together.
And then we never came out, you see?
And for three days we were there-- no food.
So you also went there.
Yes.
But that time, they left-- for three days, they left the door open, you know?
People, they were going out and coming, you know?
After three days, they closed the gates-- who was there.
And somebody was crying, and they want to go.
He said, no, you're not going from here, he said, the German.
You're going through the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] what do you call the-- what they put fire?
What do you call this-- A chimney.
Chimney, you know?
And people, they said, no, I've got a Turkish passport.
Say, show me.
So they went to the house, and they found the passport.
Three, four families, they came out from the-- from that camp.
You know?
And after three days, they put the alarm, you know, like bombing.
Like, people came to put bombs, you know?
And they took us to the-- what do you call-- to the-- what is the boat?
What do you call the-- [SPANISH]
[SPANISH]
They took us there.
And slowly, slowly-- and then, with the Germans and with the dogs, you know, they took us to the-- that port.
And then we saw three little boats, you know, where they put all the coal inside.
And we went in.
And many people, they were sick.
Many people, they-- they carried them with the [INAUDIBLE], you know?
With the [NON-ENGLISH].
With the beds.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And they put us there.
It was terrible.
They-- they-- we had no place even--
Walking.
Walking.
And I told you, they--
Huh?
No, from--
Not so far from [INAUDIBLE] to go to the port.
It was not so far, you know?
I don't know, two, three hours, you know?
Something like this.
I don't remember now.
In any case, we arrived there.
And they start putting them inside, inside.
So we arrived there.
And we want water.
There was nothing, you know?
And food and people crying, because many people, they didn't stay together.
You see, we went, the three of us, together-- Matteo, Maurizio, Djoya, and myself, we three.
Can I just ask you something?
You said two brothers went to Israel.
To Israel, yeah.
No, one died.
We were seven.
So we were four--
My sister, she was married to the Italian, I told you.
She emigrate, 1942, to Rome.
With a family.
You see?
She had four children.
We were four.
So we went to the concentration four.
We came back three.
No.
OK, but now we're now at the port, at [NON-ENGLISH].
We were three.
We were four, sorry.
Felicie, Lucia, Djoya, and Maurizio.
Four, yeah.
No, nothing.
They said--
They said, you must go to this boat.
And we didn't know where they are going to take us, you see?
And then in the nighttime they-- is to go the boat, you know?
Daytime, they were afraid in case they bombing.
You see the-- the-- the English people, they used to come and bomb-- or Americans, I don't know.
So they used to-- they were afraid to go in daytime.
We used to go in nighttime.
So daytime, we used to stop.
And it was very hot.
But the German, there were only seven in the boats.
We didn't know where they were taking.
Otherwise, we-- we could kill them, you know?
So there was a big group of you--
Yes.
700 Or 800 in each boat-- I don't know-- you see?
No, we didn't know.
So, after nearly--
I told you-- a little suitcase, what we had--
Yeah, yes.
That's why I say, it was funny.
It was-- everything was up and down, you know?
Even to go to the loo, there were-- terrible, nothing.
It was very hard there.
There was no toilets, either.
Yeah, there were toilets.
Mind, you know, so many people went there, it was full up.
And the water was not flushing.
And the headaches, from the dirt, you know?
For nearly eight days we were there in the boats.
So and then, after, we arrive in Pireo, you know?
Yes.
Yes, we were all together, yeah.
We used to help even-- we used to help the old people, you know?
Nothing.
We used to cry.
And we say, where are they taking us?
We didn't know all these things.
No.
My-- my friends-- you know, I had a Greek people.
They say, stay here, Lucia.
Lucia, stay here!
And I say, I'm going with my family.
I didn't want to leave them.
Yeah.
No, nothing.
Nothing.
At-- at at Pireo.
We were going to Pireo.
But before, we went to all the islands, you know?
And they-- they--
No, because, I told you, like, they were stopping all the time, you see?
Because of the bombing, you know?
Throwing bombs and things like this.
Yeah.
From there, we arrived at Pireo.
And in the meantime, it was so hot there.
We couldn't stand the heat.
And they say, all the women, they must sit one side and all the men, the other side.
So it was a-- a [INAUDIBLE].
The mother was dead, you see?
And the daughter was taking the father, who was very old.
Maybe he was 60, 70.
And he had a beard, you know?
Beard.
[INAUDIBLE] beard, yes?
And then, when he was walking-- he couldn't walk, because he was blind.
The daughter took him.
And they were hitting the daughter.
And--
The German was hitting the daughter and the-- that man.
[INAUDIBLE] ah, ah!
And we were crying sitting there.
Because he couldn't go alone.
He was blind.
And the daughter was [INAUDIBLE].
We used to cry.
So another German came to ask me, well, why I'm crying.
He say, I'm not German, I'm Austrian.
He was sorry.
Because-- he say, why you are here?
I say, because Jewish.
I told him, you see?
He said, I'm Austrian, not German.
He was not SS, you know?
But he's to do his army, you know?
And he was quite nice mensch him.
And after-- With you, he was nice.
Yeah.
From-- no, from all the-- when we were crying there, he asked us why we were crying and why-- why we are here.
You see?
And we told him, because we're Jewish.
They took us, the Germans.
No, he didn't know.
You see?
And after from there, they brought two lorries to put the people up.
[INAUDIBLE]
From [INAUDIBLE].
From Pireo--
--we went to Chaidari.
You see?
Yeah.
No, by-- by-- by car, you know?
They put two lorries.
Because in Pireo, when we arrived in Pireo--
--by boat.
After, they brought two lorries--
--to put the people, to take us-- I don't know-- 50, 60 miles away from Pireo.
They had a camp.
Because there were plenty people there still from Salonika.
You see?
So when we arrived there, they say, oh, you arrive here.
Now, they are going to take [INAUDIBLE] to Auschwitz.
Where is Auschwitz, we ask.
They say it's in German.
Because already they took many people.
And we were the last, they say.
Because in 1944 the war is nearly finished.
And so you arrive here.
Now, they are going to take us to Auschwitz.
So we stayed there three, four days.
And we used to speak with these people in Spanish or Greek, you know?
And after, they took us for Auschwitz.
And they put us the train.
So from-- you went by bus--
Yeah.
No, by--
By truck.
And many people, they were fat, you know?
They couldn't go up.
They used to hit the people, you know?
And you used to cry.
In the meantime, they hit!
People died there.
Many people.
Huh?
They had bastones, you know?
[NON-ENGLISH] They had-- ooh, it was very strong when they used to hit you, you know?
Yeah.
Yes, they hit me also.
Because-- [NON-ENGLISH].
They used to say, run!
They tell we could run.
But the old people, they couldn't run, like my auntie.
I was about 24, nearly-- 23 and a half. 24, say.
But I looked like 17 at the time, you know?
I was thin.
And we were happy.
And yeah.
No.
After, when we went to Chaidari--
We stayed, I told you, like, two hours, three hours.
And then we stopped there for three days.
And after, they took us again to-- to the trains where they carried the animals, you know?
The truck for the--
Nothing.
We didn't know where they're taking us, you see?
Everybody was crying, everybody-- come here, [NON-ENGLISH].
And everybody, we went through there, you see?
Yeah, the leaders.
Yeah.
And they put there, there is the-- there were a barrel of water, something like this.
And we sit in the floor with the suitcases what we had--
--with us, in the truck.
And every-- the train, it was already full.
It was, like, a track for the animals-- what do you call them.
Cattle train, you know?
And everybody sitting the floor, and it was dirty and everything.
And then we had the journey--
Maybe, I don't know, four, five, six, or eight days?
I don't remember, really.
Because myself, these days, I'm getting for forget everything, you know?
I don't remember.
Maybe two weeks, yeah.
Yeah, they used to stop for water.
And they used to come--
Water-- drinking water.
No.
We-- we.
You see?
They used to take, [NON-ENGLISH] Outside!
We used to go down.
And the waters of the toilets, you know, they used to go up, the German, and throw away the water, the dirty water on top of you.
Wee, you know?
Oh, the buckets full of--
The buckets of-- yeah, they used to throw.
And it was very hard in the train, you know?
Yeah, on our people.
We used to come out from the truck--
From the train.
And they used to throw that water, dirty, on top of you.
Over you.
In the meantime, the young fellows, they used to bring the water--
--to put in the train.
I don't know.
They used to have a-- a barrel.
And these young fellows, Jewish fellows, they used to take up on the train--
Yeah, the drinking water.
No food.
I think-- I remember they put some watermelon.
[INAUDIBLE] I don't-- a piece of--
Dried things, yeah.
And then we were there in the train.
All of a sudden, it start going, the train.
No.
They-- we stop and the-- the every thing.
Like, [INAUDIBLE], we stop.
And I don't know where we stop in any case.
There were Red Cross.
They used to bring plenty food, never gave us nothing.
Never.
You see?
So the people, they were throwing things-- little apples, a piece of bread.
Never mind, they gave us bread.
Who was throwing that?
The people that were there.
Maybe they-- the civilians from countries which we used to pass.
They used to throw it in the train.
Yeah, with the thing.
Yeah.
And the bread, inside there were little knives, you know?
To cut the wood, to escape.
But we didn't know nothing-- how can you leave?
Little knives they put in the bread--
--to help you running away when they stop the train-- to cut the train pieces, you know?
And then [INAUDIBLE] through.
But we didn't leave the family.
No.
No.
And that's all.
And then, after-- after a few-- it was a week or two weeks, I don't remember--
Yeah, plenty.
Yeah, plenty.
[INAUDIBLE]
And the ladies, they were expecting, also-- I beg your pardon?
No, in my-- in my train there, nobody died.
Plenty, they were fainting.
But in the other trains, you know, the people they had-- people that were expecting, people, old people-- I think-- I knew a man they throw him in the sea.
Near [INAUDIBLE], you know?
Mr.
Levy.
And one, he died in Athens.
Mr.
[INAUDIBLE], he died there with the heats.
But all that was, you know?
When we arrive in Auschwitz, it was in the morning.
We were so happy to-- oh, thank god, we arrive to stretch our legs.
And we saw so many people there.
We didn't know what it was.
And all [INAUDIBLE], what do you call these, uh--
[SPANISH]
Yeah.
The fence, yeah.
You see?
And then, when we were there, we were happy there.
And then, in the meantime, there were the Germans, the SS, with the dogs.
And they should take one from here, one from there.
So my auntie said, come with me.
Help me.
So my sister, she passed already with the young people.
The one, she died there.
She said, Lucia, come this way!
Come this way!
So when I went, the other German saw me arrive from the-- that line, at there.
He hit me in the face, you know?
And then I was happy with them, with my sister, the three of us.
And my brother was separate, because-- so when we went to the camp where they put these [SPANISH]-- what you call?
The-- yes, the fence.
Fence.
And we saw my brother was in Lager B.
We were in Lager A, you know?
No.
And then when they start crying, we cry, you know?
Ay, ay!
The other people say, I want my mother.
I want my father.
You know, I didn't have my father and mother, died in Rhodes, you see-- but the other people.
So the Salonika people, who knew, they took them two years before, you know?
And they say, oh, my-- my [INAUDIBLE].
Don't cry, because your mother and father, they're already in the crematorium.
And see, look there.
This is smoke where is the people burn, you know?
